medications.drug_name: Simple analgesia (paracetamol) if the rash or legs are uncomfortable
medications.indication_rationale: Mild cutaneous IgA vasculitis needs no specific drug therapy; simple analgesia is optional symptomatic relief only. No dose is specified because the source note prescribes none — a dose must not be invented.

NSAIDs should be avoided while renal involvement is not yet excluded — they can worsen renal perfusion and mask/aggravate GI bleeding in IgA vasculitis. Recommending them here is an error of commission.
contraindications_in_this_case: Possible occult renal involvement not yet excluded by urinalysis

medications.indication_rationale: Systemic steroids are NOT indicated for mild skin-limited IgA vasculitis; they are reserved for significant GI or renal involvement under specialist guidance. Starting steroids for a cutaneous-only presentation is an error of commission. No dose is provided (none in source).

The patient is well and afebrile and this is not an infective rash; empirical antibiotics from a telehealth service are not the answer. If meningococcaemia were genuinely suspected (fever + spreading rash + toxicity) the correct action is emergency escalation (000/ED) where parenteral antibiotics are given, not a remote prescription.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest and elevate the legs on a pillow when sitting or lying over the next 48 hours to reduce hydrostatic pressure in the small leg vessels.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Leg elevation and reduced dependent pressure limit further purpura and aid resolution in cutaneous IgA vasculitis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid prolonged standing and strenuous activity for a couple of days.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prolonged standing increases dependent capillary pressure and can drive new crops of purpura.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Check the colour of the urine daily and watch for new abdominal pain, spreading rash or fever until reviewed and during the following weeks.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Renal and GI involvement can appear after the skin signs; home self-monitoring supports early detection between formal urinalysis checks.

patient_education_points.point: This looks most like a temporary inflammation of the tiny blood vessels in the skin, commonly triggered by a recent cold — but a new rash that does not fade when pressed always needs to be checked in person the same day before that reassurance is safe.
It is NOT the dangerous bacterial infection you are worried about, based on you being well and having no fever — however that can only be confirmed by an in-person check today, not over chat.
A urine test and a blood-pressure check are important today and again over the coming weeks, because this condition can occasionally affect the kidneys even when you feel well.
It usually settles on its own over days to a few weeks; keep your legs elevated and rest.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment; then GP review in 3-5 days with repeat urinalysis and blood-pressure check, and further urinalysis/BP surveillance over the following weeks.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Urine dipstick (blood/protein), blood pressure, rash progression, and any new GI, joint or systemic symptoms; ongoing renal surveillance because adult-onset disease carries higher renal risk.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any fever, rapidly spreading rash, severe abdominal pain, vomiting, cola/tea-coloured urine, reduced urine output, mucosal bleeding, or feeling systemically unwell.

must_include_items: Recognise that a new non-blanching (petechial/purpuric) rash cannot be safely cleared over telehealth and requires urgent same-day in-person assessment
State that the in-person assessment must include a urine dipstick, blood-pressure check and a full blood count to exclude meningococcaemia, thrombocytopenia and nephritis
Provide explicit safety-netting for deterioration (fever, spreading rash, severe abdominal pain, cola-coloured urine, mucosal bleeding)
Advise supportive care — leg elevation and rest
Arrange ongoing renal surveillance (urinalysis + blood pressure over the following weeks)
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to an urgent care clinic or same-day GP rather than a specific practice
Acknowledging the patient's fear of meningococcaemia while explaining why urgent in-person assessment (not 000) is the calibrated response for a well, afebrile patient
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange same-day in-person assessment / urinalysis
Failing to safety-net for GI, renal and systemic-infection red flags
Not recognising the telehealth limitation and reassuring the patient home remotely
errors_of_commission: Providing definitive remote reassurance that the rash is benign without in-person assessment
Prescribing systemic corticosteroids for skin-limited disease
Recommending NSAIDs while renal involvement is unexcluded
Over-triaging a well, afebrile, stable adult to a 000 ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising that the non-blanching rash needs urgent same-day in-person assessment (urinalysis/BP/FBC) and providing red-flag safety-netting. Remote reassurance-and-discharge, OR omitting the renal/infection safety-net, is a failing response even if the benign diagnosis is otherwise named.
